retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Antioxidant administration during radiotherapy has been shown to increase efficacy and decrease the side effect profile. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of acute radiation enteritis often self-resolve within weeks of supportive treatment with antimotility agents and good water intake; surgery is rarely needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Proctitis -- Treatment / Management -- Chronic Proctitis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who develop chronic proctitis over the ensuing months to years after treatment have several medical treatment options. Evidence for the various treatments ranges from anecdotal to small randomized prospective trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If malnourishment is suspected, patients should be checked for decrease intake and/or malabsorption. Surgical removal in patients with mechanical malabsorption does not relieve symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Esophagitis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Many agents have been studies to prevent radiation-induced esophagitis. Amifostine, L glutamine, and NSAIDS have all been investigated with conflicting data. The key to prevention is awareness and obtaining a nutritional consult early in the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Proctitis -- Treatment / Management -- Primary Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic medical therapies such as sucralfate, amifostine, and misoprostol have failed to demonstrate a reduction in the risk of proctitis in patients undergoing pelvic radiotherapy. However, data suggests that selenium supplementation taken during radiation treatments may reduce the risk of grade 2+ diarrhea. A small randomized trial of patients undergoing pelvic radiotherapy for uterine and cervical cancer taking 500 mg of selenium versus placebo demonstrated a significant reduction in the rate of grade 2+ diarrhea (20.5% vs 44.5%). [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Mild bleeding and minimal symptoms do not require further treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Ionizing Radiation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Acute radiation sickness occurs at high-dose (1-12 Gray, Gy) exposures. [8] Acute radiation poisoning is a clinical condition encompassing 4 organ systems, leading to the development of specific subsyndromes: Hematopoietic syndrome Gastrointestinal subsyndrome Neurovascular subsyndrome Cutaneous subsyndrome Countermeasures of potential benefit include Cytokines Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation Fluoroquinolones Bowel decontamination Serotonin receptor antagonists Loperamide Enteral nutrition Topical steroids Antihistamines Antibiotics Surgical excision/grafting for skin changes [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Oral antibiotics are given to patients suspected of having bacterial overgrowth, along with vitamins and electrolytes replacement if needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Mucositis -- Treatment / Management -- Antiinflamatory Agents
retrieved_docs.content: The MASCC/ISOO guidelines recommend benzydamine mouthwash for preventing oral mucositis in head and neck cancer patients receiving less than 50 Gy radiation dose (moderate dose) and in those receiving radiotherapy-chemotherapy (RT-CT). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biologic, Chemical, and Radiation Terrorism Review -- Issues of Concern -- Biologic Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Signs/Symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and severe dehydration.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Esophagitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment is supportive care with adequate hydration and nutrition intake. Amifostine, an organic triphosphate, has been studied and shown to act as a radioprotector. It is thought that this medication acts as an antioxidant to neutralize the free-oxygen radicals produced during ionizing radiation. A study performed to look at the effects of amifostine in patients with advanced non-small-cell lung cancer showed improvement of swallowing dysfunction but no effect on reducing rates of esophagitis in severe cases. Another potential radioprotectant, glutamine, has been associated with reduced rates of esophagitis. A small prospective study in non-small cell lung cancer patients showed 49% reported no esophagitis when treated prophylactically with glutamine powder. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and other drugs have been studied but have not proven effective. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: As a consequence of chronic radiation, Patients who have enteritis leading to the formation of intestinal strictures, fistulas, and perforations require surgical procedures. One study showed high postoperative morbidity (74.8%) after the first surgical procedure. Many patients required parenteral nutrition after surgery. Data indicates repeated surgical procedures may be needed; they do not seem to increase the morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Ongoing studies have shown an increased risk of malignancy as a late consequence of pelvic radiation.

retrieved_docs.content: Category A: High Risk ANTHRAX: CUTANEOUS, GASTROINTESTINAL, AND INHALATIONAL

retrieved_docs.title: Biologic, Chemical, and Radiation Terrorism Review -- Issues of Concern -- Biologic Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Ricin is a toxic lectin produced by the castor oil plant and found in the seeds. A dose the size of a few grains of table salt can kill a human. The LD 50 is about 22 micrograms per kilogram of body weight. Injection or inhalation is more toxic than oral ingestion. Signs/Symptoms: Toxic if inhaled, injected, or ingested. It causes abdominal pain, coughing, diarrhea, fever, nausea, necrotizing pneumonia, pulmonary edema, shock, tracheobronchitis, vomiting, and weakness. Treatment: Charcoal lavage and supportive care. Biologic Warfare: Several countries have considered or attempted to weaponized ricin. Given ricin's extreme toxicity it is noteworthy that the production of the toxin is rather difficult to limit. The castor bean plant from which ricin is derived is a common ornamental and can be grown at home without any special care. STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS (Enterotoxin Type B)

retrieved_docs.content: B [Preparations] are pharmaceutical or nutraceutical formulations whose parent compound is beta‑tocopherol, a specific isoform of vitamin E. By using beta‑tocopherol as the foundational ingredient, B [Preparations] leverage its antioxidant properties to enhance therapeutic efficacy, improve stability of active ingredients, and contribute to overall patient health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: In radiation biology, each qualifier can modulate, enhance, mitigate, or otherwise influence physiological responses to ionizing and non‑ionizing radiation. Hypoxanthine’s PARP‑inhibitory activity specifically suppresses DNA‑repair mechanisms, affecting cellular recovery after radiation‑induced damage. Recognizing these agents as radiation modifiers informs the design of protective strategies, therapeutic regimens, and risk assessments for both therapeutic radiation exposure and accidental radiation incidents. Collectively, the inclusion of these permissible qualifiers facilitates standardized manufacturing, regulatory compliance, targeted therapeutic development, and a comprehensive understanding of immunological, metabolic, and radiobiological processes, thereby improving patient care and advancing biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: The community identifies a set of pharmacologic and biochemical agents—including HIV, antitumor antibiotics, bendroflumethiazid, diosmin, galantamin, glucose, gum karaya, Na‑K‑Cl symporter inhibitors, clotrimazole, vitamin B12, glycyrhetinic acid, nitric oxide, pentagastrin, and silymarin—as having documented interactions with ionizing radiation. These agents can alter radiation‑induced biological responses by modulating cellular protection, DNA repair, or radiosensitization, making them permissible qualifiers in radiation‑effect assessments. The same compounds are also recognized as relevant qualifiers within the domain of chemistry, reflecting their importance in chemical research, pharmaceutical formulation, and interdisciplinary study of infectious agents, pharmacology, and biochemistry. Understanding these relationships supports safe therapeutic use and informs patient care in contexts involving radiation exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term used in radiotherapy to denote specific clinical conditions that are considered permissible or relevant when evaluating a patient for treatment. The allowed qualifiers for radiotherapy include respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest in adults, as well as عدوى بفيروس معوي (infection with an enteric virus). Recognizing these qualifiers helps clinicians assess patient suitability for radiotherapy, anticipate potential complications, and tailor therapeutic plans accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1773
retrieved_docs.content: Amfotericin B, anthralin, anti‑parathyroid agents, bacterial vaccines, cetylpyridinium, dienestrol, diflunisal, diltiazem, insulin Lispro, paclitaxel, piracetam, iron(III) sulfate (iron saccharate), sodium selenite, orlistat, selective estrogen‑receptor modulators (SERMs) and silymarin are all designated as permissible qualifiers in the context of urine (moč), meaning that the presence, measurement or reporting of these agents may be relevant to urinary testing or interpretation. The same set of agents—bacterial vaccines, cetylpyridinium, dienestrol, diflunisal, diltiazem, insulin Lispro, orlistat, paclitaxel, piracetam, iron saccharate, SERMs, sodium selenite and silymarin—are also recognized as allowed qualifiers for the effects of radiation, indicating that they may influence, modify, or need to be considered when assessing radiation‑induced outcomes. In addition, HIV infection, amfotericin B, anthralin and anti‑parathyroid agents are each specifically associated with radiation effects, highlighting a direct link between these conditions or therapies and radiation‑related biological responses. Collectively, these qualifier relationships underscore the clinical importance of accounting for a broad spectrum of drugs, vaccines, antiseptics, hormonal agents, anti‑inflammatories, cardiovascular drugs, insulin analogues, chemotherapeutics, cognitive enhancers, mineral supplements, receptor modulators and hepatoprotective herbs both in urinary diagnostics and in the management of radiation‑induced effects, thereby informing therapeutic decision‑making and patient care.
